trust.prompt: A person is considering lending their specialized camera equipment to a colleague from a different department for an important project. The equipment is crucial for the project's success, and the colleague has a reputation for being responsible, but the person has never worked with them directly. The decision to lend the equipment hinges on whether the person trusts the colleague to handle it carefully and return it in good condition.

A group of friends are planning a group vacation and need to decide who will book the accommodations. One friend offers to take care of it, but the others are hesitant because they have never traveled together before and are not sure if they can trust this friend to make arrangements that suit everyone's needs. The decision to trust this friend with the booking affects the overall success of the trip.

A new employee is given a task that requires collaboration with a team member who has a history of being uncooperative. The new employee must decide whether to trust this team member to provide necessary information and support, or to spend extra time verifying everything independently, which could delay the project. The decision to trust the team member affects not only the project's timeline but also the new employee's relationships with their colleagues.

A person is asked to provide a professional reference for a former classmate who is now applying for a job in the same field. The person is not closely acquainted with the classmate's work but is aware of their good reputation. The decision to provide a reference depends on whether the person trusts the classmate to have accurately represented their skills and experience.

A community center is considering partnering with a local business to offer joint programs. The business has a good reputation, but the community center has never worked with them before. The decision to trust the business and move forward with the partnership affects the community center's ability to offer new services to its members.

A researcher is deciding whether to share preliminary data with a potential collaborator from another institution. The collaborator has a good track record of producing high-quality work, but the researcher is not sure if they can trust this person to keep the data confidential and not use it for their own benefit. The decision to share the data affects the potential for future collaboration and the advancement of the research.

A person is thinking of joining a carpool with coworkers who live in the same neighborhood. The person does not know these coworkers well outside of work but is considering the environmental and financial benefits of carpooling. The decision to trust these coworkers to be reliable and respectful carpool partners affects the person's daily commute and overall job satisfaction.

A person is considering lending their specialized camera equipment to a new colleague for an upcoming photoshoot, with the understanding that the colleague will return the equipment in good condition and possibly offer assistance on a future project. The decision hinges on whether the colleague will reciprocate the favor and take proper care of the equipment.

A family is deciding whether to allow their neighbors, who they have a cordial but not close relationship with, to borrow their lawn mower in exchange for future help with snow shoveling during the winter months. The family must weigh the potential benefits of reciprocal help against the risk of damage to their property.

A freelance writer is offered a collaboration opportunity with another writer they met online, where they would work together on a project and split the profits. The writer must decide whether to trust their new partner to contribute equally to the project and uphold their end of the agreement.

A community center is considering partnering with a local business to offer joint programs, with the business providing resources and the community center providing space and expertise. The community center must trust that the business will follow through on their commitments and provide the necessary support.

A person is thinking of joining a carpool with coworkers who they don't know very well, where they would take turns driving and share the costs of gas and maintenance. The person must decide whether to trust their coworkers to fulfill their driving obligations and contribute fairly to the shared expenses.

A group of friends are planning a group vacation and are considering renting a large house together, with each person contributing to the cost and taking turns planning activities. The friends must trust each other to pay their share and participate in the planning process.

A small business owner is deciding whether to trust a new supplier to deliver goods on time and in good condition, in exchange for a lower price and potential long-term partnership. The business owner must weigh the potential benefits of a reliable supplier against the risk of delayed or damaged shipments.

A person is considering lending their rare, expensive book to a new coworker for a few weeks, despite having only interacted with them briefly in the office. The coworker seems genuinely interested in the book's topic and promises to return it in good condition, but there's a risk it could get damaged or lost. Not lending the book would mean missing an opportunity to build a connection with the coworker.

A family is deciding whether to trust a recommended, unlicensed babysitter who has been vouched for by a trusted family friend, to watch their young children for an evening. The sitter seems experienced and kind, but the family has never met them before, and there's a risk that the sitter might not be able to handle an emergency. Hiring a licensed sitter would be more expensive and might not be available on short notice.

A group of friends are planning a group vacation and need to decide whether to trust one friend to book all the accommodations and activities without getting everyone's explicit approval first. The friend is usually reliable, but there's a risk that they might make choices that not everyone will like, or that something will go wrong with the bookings. Having everyone approve each decision would take a lot of time and might delay the planning process.

A researcher is considering sharing their unpublished data with a colleague from another institution, who has requested it for a collaborative project. The colleague seems reputable and has a good track record of producing high-quality research, but there's a risk that they might use the data for their own benefit or share it with others without permission. Not sharing the data would mean missing an opportunity for a potentially valuable collaboration.

A community is deciding whether to trust a new, untested community organization to manage a local park renovation project, which would involve handling a significant budget and making decisions about the park's design. The organization seems enthusiastic and has a good vision for the project, but there's a risk that they might not have the necessary expertise or experience to handle the project successfully. Hiring a more established organization would be more expensive and might not bring the same level of community engagement.

A person is considering trusting a friend's recommendation to hire a specific contractor to do some home repairs, despite not having met the contractor before. The friend has used the contractor in the past and recommends them highly, but there's a risk that the contractor might not do a good job or might overcharge for their services. Getting multiple bids from different contractors would take more time and effort, but would provide more assurance about the quality and price of the work.

A team of entrepreneurs is deciding whether to trust a potential investor who has expressed interest in their startup, but wants to remain anonymous. The investor seems to have a good understanding of the business and is offering a significant amount of funding, but there's a risk that they might have ulterior motives or might try to exert too much control over the company. Not accepting the investment would mean giving up a potentially valuable opportunity for growth, but would also avoid the risk of losing control or autonomy.

A person is considering lending their rare book to a new colleague for a research project, and the colleague promises to return it in the same condition. The person must decide whether to trust the colleague's promise and lend the book, or to spend time making a digital copy of the book's contents to protect their investment.

A family is planning a vacation and needs to rent a house from an individual they have never met. The owner has provided references and seems friendly, but the family is unsure if they should trust the owner to provide a clean and safe living space. They must decide whether to trust the owner and send a deposit, or to spend more time researching and verifying the owner's reputation.

A group of friends are planning a potluck dinner and one of the friends, who is new to the group, offers to bring a dish that requires a special ingredient. The others must decide whether to trust that the new friend will bring the correct ingredient, or to spend extra money buying a backup ingredient to ensure the dish turns out well.

A person is selling their used car and a potential buyer, who seems honest and knowledgeable, asks to take the car for a test drive without leaving a deposit. The seller must decide whether to trust the buyer and let them take the car, or to insist on a deposit to protect themselves from potential theft or damage.

A team of researchers is collaborating on a project and one of the team members, who is working remotely, claims to have completed a critical task. The others must decide whether to trust that the task is done correctly, or to spend time reviewing and verifying the work to ensure its quality.

A person is considering hiring a freelance writer to create content for their website, and the writer has provided samples of their work and seems competent. However, the person has never worked with the writer before and is unsure if they can trust them to deliver high-quality content on time. They must decide whether to trust the writer and hire them, or to spend more time interviewing and verifying the writer's credentials.

A community center is planning an event and a local business owner has offered to donate food and drinks for the attendees. The center's staff must decide whether to trust that the business owner will follow through on their promise, or to spend extra time and resources finding alternative donors to ensure the event's success.

A person is considering lending their rare book to a new colleague for a research project, which would require them to trust that the colleague will handle the book with care and return it on time, in order to build a positive working relationship.

A family is deciding whether to allow their child to stay overnight at a friend's house for the first time, which would require them to trust the friend's family to provide a safe environment, in exchange for the potential benefits of increased socialization and independence for their child.

A team of entrepreneurs is evaluating whether to share their business plan with a potential investor, who has a reputation for being trustworthy, but still poses a risk of stealing their idea, in order to secure funding and take their business to the next level.

A homeowner is considering hiring a contractor who was referred by a friend, but has no online reviews or formal certifications, which would require them to trust that the contractor will do quality work and not take advantage of them, in exchange for the potential benefits of a personalized recommendation and lower costs.

A student is deciding whether to trust a classmate to collaborate on a group project, despite having had negative experiences with group work in the past, in order to achieve a better grade and learn from the classmate's strengths.

A community center is evaluating whether to allow a new, untested organization to use their facilities for a event, which would require them to trust that the organization will respect their space and follow their rules, in exchange for the potential benefits of increased community engagement and diversity.

A researcher is considering sharing their preliminary findings with a colleague from a different institution, who has a reputation for being a rigorous and respectful scholar, but still poses a risk of scooping their research, in order to get feedback and improve the quality of their work.

risktaking.prompt: A family is planning a vacation and must decide between booking a package tour with a reputable company or organizing the trip themselves, which could be more cost-effective but also increases the risk of mistakes and unforeseen expenses.

A freelancer is offered a chance to work with a new client on a project that aligns with their long-term career goals, but the client's payment history is uncertain, and the freelancer would have to turn down a shorter, well-paid project to take on the new opportunity.

A group of friends want to start a small business together and must decide whether to invest their own savings or seek funding from a venture capitalist, which would provide more resources but also mean giving up some control over the company's direction.

A person is considering switching to a new, environmentally friendly energy provider for their home, which would require a significant upfront investment but could lead to long-term savings and a reduced carbon footprint.

A team leader must decide whether to delegate a critical task to a new team member who shows potential but lacks experience, or to assign it to a more seasoned team member who is already overloaded with work, which could impact the team's overall performance and morale.

A couple is planning their retirement and must choose between investing in a diversified portfolio with a potentially higher return or opting for a more conservative investment strategy that provides greater stability but might not keep pace with inflation.

A small business owner is faced with the decision to expand their operations to a new location, which would require taking on additional debt and staff, or to maintain the current size and focus on consolidating their existing market share, which would be less risky but might limit future growth opportunities.

A person is considering inviting a new colleague to join their project team, despite having limited experience working together. The new colleague has skills that could greatly benefit the project, but also has a reputation for being difficult to work with, which could disrupt team dynamics.

A family is deciding whether to host an international student for a semester, which would provide a unique cultural exchange opportunity but also requires significant adjustments to their daily routines and living space.

A group of friends are planning a weekend trip and one of them suggests trying a new, unconventional mode of transportation that could be exciting but also poses safety risks and unpredictable delays.

A community leader is weighing the pros and cons of partnering with a local business to organize a charity event, which could increase visibility and fundraising potential but also risks being seen as overly commercialized.

A researcher is considering collaborating with a colleague from a different field on a groundbreaking project, which could lead to innovative discoveries but also requires navigating unfamiliar methodologies and potential conflicts over authorship.

A team of entrepreneurs is deciding whether to pitch their startup idea to a prominent investor, who has a reputation for being tough but also has the resources to take their business to the next level, or to play it safe and seek funding from a less prominent source.

A student is thinking about speaking up in a large lecture class to challenge a statement made by the instructor, which could lead to a valuable discussion and clarification but also risks being perceived as confrontational or disruptive.

You're on a solo hiking trip and come across a fast-moving river that you need to cross. You can either try to ford the river immediately, which could be dangerous due to the strong current, or take a few hours to hike upstream to a safer crossing point, which would add significant time to your journey.

You've been invited to join a friend on a challenging rock climbing route with exposed sections and no safety nets. If you succeed, you'll have a thrilling experience and a great story to share, but if you fail, you could get seriously injured.

You're part of a search and rescue team responding to a call in a remote area with dense forest and steep terrain. You can either follow established protocols and search in a systematic pattern, which may take longer to locate the missing person, or take a more direct but riskier route that could potentially find the person faster but also increases the chance of getting lost or injured.

You're a member of a competitive sailing team, and during a critical race, you need to decide whether to take a riskier route that could give you an advantage over your competitors or stick to a safer route that is more likely to result in a middle-of-the-pack finish.

You're on a wilderness survival course and are given the option to try to start a fire using a challenging method, such as friction, which could be rewarding if successful but may leave you without a fire for the night if you fail, or use a more reliable but less challenging method, such as matches.

You're part of a group planning a charity event, and one of the options is to organize a high-risk, high-reward activity, such as a sponsored skydive, which could attract a lot of attention and donations but also poses significant safety risks, or choose a safer, more conventional activity, such as a bake sale, which is less likely to result in a large payoff.

You're a beginner surfer, and you're faced with the decision to paddle out to catch a large wave that's beyond your skill level, which could be an exhilarating experience if you succeed but also increases the risk of wiping out and getting injured, or stick to smaller waves that are more within your comfort zone.

A team leader must decide whether to delegate a critical project task to a new team member who shows promise but lacks experience, potentially risking the project's success, or to assign it to a more seasoned team member who is already overloaded with work.

A person is considering investing in a friend's startup, which has a unique product but faces intense competition, and must weigh the potential for high returns against the risk of losing the investment and potentially straining the friendship.

A manager must choose between two suppliers for a key component: one is a well-established company with a higher price but reliable quality, while the other is a newer company offering a lower price but with some uncertainty about their ability to meet the required standards.

A researcher is deciding whether to pursue a novel research direction that could lead to a groundbreaking discovery but may not yield results for several years, or to focus on a more conventional research topic that is likely to produce publishable results sooner but may not have as significant an impact.

A small business owner must decide whether to expand their operations to a new location, which could increase revenue but also increases costs and the risk of failure, or to maintain the current scale and focus on consolidating their existing customer base.

A group of friends are planning a wilderness expedition and must decide whether to take a more challenging route that offers breathtaking scenery but also higher risks of accidents and inclement weather, or to choose a safer route that is less demanding but may not be as rewarding.

A department head must decide whether to implement a new, untested technology that could significantly improve efficiency but may also disrupt existing workflows and require significant retraining of staff, or to stick with the current systems and processes that are familiar but may be less efficient.

A family business owner must decide whether to invest in a new, untested technology to improve their production process, which could lead to significant cost savings but also risks disrupting their current operations and potentially losing valuable customers if it fails.

A researcher is considering whether to collaborate with a new, inexperienced team member on a high-stakes project, which could bring fresh perspectives and ideas but also increases the risk of errors and delays if the team member is not up to the task.

A manager is faced with the decision of whether to give a second chance to an employee who has made a serious mistake, which could demonstrate empathy and loyalty but also risks repeating the mistake and damaging the team's morale and performance.

A entrepreneur is weighing the pros and cons of launching a new product line in a highly competitive market, which could lead to significant revenue growth but also risks cannibalizing sales from existing products and damaging the company's brand reputation if it fails.

A team leader must decide whether to take a calculated risk and delegate a critical task to a junior team member, which could help with their development and growth but also increases the risk of failure and negative consequences if the task is not completed successfully.

A small business owner is considering whether to expand their operations to a new location, which could lead to increased revenue and market share but also risks overextending resources and damaging the business's financial stability if the new location does not perform as expected.

A department head is faced with the decision of whether to implement a new, innovative policy that could lead to significant improvements in efficiency and effectiveness but also risks resistance and pushback from employees and stakeholders who are accustomed to the existing way of doing things.

patience.prompt: A family is planning a vacation and has the option to book a popular resort immediately, but it's expensive and not their first choice. They can wait a few weeks to see if their preferred resort becomes available at a lower price, which would require adjusting their travel dates. Waiting means tolerating uncertainty for a potentially better experience.

A researcher is working on a project and has the opportunity to publish a paper quickly, but it would require omitting some important data and analysis. They can wait a few months to collect more data and conduct further analysis, which would strengthen their findings and increase the paper's impact. Waiting means delaying professional recognition in exchange for producing higher-quality work.

A group of friends want to start a business together and have the option to launch quickly with a basic product. However, they can also wait a few months to develop a more innovative and polished product, which would require investing more time and effort. Waiting means postponing potential revenue in exchange for a stronger market position and higher long-term growth.

A person is considering buying a new car and has the option to purchase a model that's available immediately, but it's not the most fuel-efficient or environmentally friendly. They can wait a few months for a new model to be released, which would be more eco-friendly and cost-effective in the long run. Waiting means tolerating their current vehicle's limitations for a potentially better long-term outcome.

A team is working on a complex project and has the option to deliver a partial solution quickly, which would meet the immediate needs of some stakeholders. However, they can also wait a few weeks to develop a more comprehensive solution that addresses the needs of all stakeholders, which would require coordinating with more people and gathering additional feedback. Waiting means delaying short-term satisfaction in exchange for a more sustainable and inclusive outcome.

A person is learning a new language and has the option to focus on basic conversation skills, which would allow them to communicate quickly with native speakers. However, they can also wait a few months to develop a stronger foundation in grammar and vocabulary, which would enable them to understand more complex texts and communicate more effectively in the long run. Waiting means postponing immediate communication in exchange for deeper understanding and fluency.

A community is planning a public event and has the option to organize a simple gathering quickly, which would be easy to execute and require minimal resources. However, they can also wait a few months to plan a more elaborate and engaging event, which would require more time, effort, and coordination with various stakeholders. Waiting means tolerating a shorter-term outcome for a potentially more impactful and memorable experience.

You're a member of a close-knit book club where everyone takes turns hosting meetings. You can either spend the next few weekends preparing an elaborate, themed dinner for your upcoming hosting duty, which will likely impress your friends and create lasting memories, or you can quickly prepare a simple meal and use the extra time to catch up on other hobbies. The elaborate dinner will require significant time and effort but will be a unique experience for your friends.

You've recently joined a recreational sports team and are eager to contribute to the team's success. You can focus on developing a specific skill that will take several practices to master but will significantly improve your performance in upcoming games, or you can work on a quick fix that will give you a minor advantage in the next game but won't provide long-term benefits. The team's coach has expressed appreciation for players who invest time in skill development.

You're planning a surprise birthday party for a family member and need to decide on the entertainment. You can hire a professional musician who will require a few weeks' notice to prepare a customized performance, or you can opt for a more generic, readily available entertainment option that can be booked on short notice. The customized performance will be more meaningful and memorable but requires waiting and planning ahead.

You're working on a group project with classmates and have the opportunity to take on a leadership role. You can spend extra time creating a comprehensive project plan that will help the team work more efficiently and effectively in the long run, or you can focus on completing your individual tasks quickly and let someone else handle the planning. The project plan will take time to develop but will save time and reduce stress for the team in the future.

You've been tasked with organizing a corporate event and need to decide on the catering. You can choose a reputable, high-end catering service that will require a minimum notice period to prepare a customized menu, or you can opt for a faster, more affordable option that may not impress your clients as much. The high-end catering service will provide a unique and memorable experience but requires waiting and planning ahead.

You're a freelance writer working on a long-term project with a flexible deadline. You can spend extra time researching and outlining to create a high-quality, engaging piece that will likely attract more readers and lead to future opportunities, or you can quickly write and submit a decent article to meet the immediate deadline and free up time for other projects. The high-quality piece will take longer to write but will have more impact and benefit your career in the long run.

You're part of a neighborhood watch program and have the opportunity to participate in a community safety initiative. You can attend a series of training sessions over the next few months to become a certified community safety leader, which will enable you to make a more significant impact and contribute to the community's long-term safety, or you can focus on shorter-term, more visible tasks that will give you a sense of immediate accomplishment but may not lead to lasting change. The training sessions will require a significant time commitment but will provide a valuable skillset and recognition within the community.

A family is planning a vacation and can choose between booking a popular resort now, which has limited availability, or waiting a few months to see if a new, potentially better resort opens up in the same area. The new resort might offer more amenities and activities, but there's a risk it won't be ready on time. Waiting means potentially getting a better experience, but also risking that the new resort doesn't meet expectations.

A researcher is working on a project and has the option to publish a preliminary paper now, which would give them a quick publication credit, or to wait and continue collecting data to publish a more comprehensive paper later. The comprehensive paper would likely have more impact and be more cited, but waiting means delaying the immediate recognition and potential career benefits.

A group of friends are deciding how to spend their weekend and can choose between attending a concert now, which would be a fun but potentially expensive and fleeting experience, or planning a weekend trip for a few months from now, which would require more planning and saving but could create longer-lasting memories. Waiting means potentially having a more meaningful experience, but also risking that the trip doesn't come together as planned.

A small business owner is considering whether to invest in a new marketing campaign now, which would give them a quick boost in sales, or to wait and focus on developing a new product line, which would take longer to pay off but could lead to more sustainable growth. Waiting means potentially missing out on short-term sales, but also risking that the new product line isn't successful.

A student is deciding whether to take a break from their studies now and travel, which would give them a chance to relax and recharge, or to wait and continue studying to get better grades, which would potentially lead to better job opportunities and higher earning potential. Waiting means potentially missing out on a unique travel experience, but also risking burnout and decreased productivity.

A team is working on a project and has the option to launch a minimum viable product now, which would give them quick feedback and revenue, or to wait and continue developing the product to make it more polished and feature-rich, which would take longer but could lead to higher customer satisfaction and retention. Waiting means potentially missing out on early revenue, but also risking that the product isn't competitive if launched too early.

A community is deciding how to allocate its resources and can choose between fixing a pressing infrastructure issue now, which would provide immediate relief, or investing in a long-term sustainability project, which would take longer to pay off but could lead to significant environmental benefits and cost savings. Waiting means potentially leaving the immediate issue unaddressed, but also risking that the sustainability project doesn't achieve its intended goals.

A family is planning a vacation and can either book a more expensive but highly-rated resort now, or wait for a potential discount on a similar resort in a few weeks, which would allow them to use the saved money for additional activities during the trip. Waiting means tolerating uncertainty about availability in exchange for potential extra experiences.

A researcher is working on a project and can either submit a preliminary paper to a conference immediately, or spend more time refining the research and submitting it to a more prestigious journal later, which could lead to greater recognition and more opportunities in the long run. Delaying submission sacrifices immediate visibility in exchange for potentially higher future impact.

A friend is considering buying a new bike and can either purchase a cheaper model now, or wait for a few months to save up for a more expensive but high-quality bike that will last longer and require less maintenance. Waiting means putting off the pleasure of having a new bike in exchange for a better long-term investment.

A team is working on a project with a tight deadline and can either take a break to celebrate a milestone now, or put in extra hours to ensure the project is completed to the highest standard, which could lead to greater client satisfaction and more business in the future. Taking a break now would sacrifice potential future benefits in exchange for immediate relaxation and celebration.

A person is learning a new language and can either focus on basic conversation skills now, or spend more time studying grammar and vocabulary, which would lead to greater proficiency and more opportunities for travel and cultural exchange in the future. Focusing on basics now would sacrifice long-term language skills in exchange for short-term communication abilities.

A community group is planning an event and can either book a popular but expensive venue now, or wait for a potentially cheaper option to become available later, which would allow them to allocate more resources to other aspects of the event. Waiting means tolerating uncertainty about the venue in exchange for potentially more resources for the event itself.

A entrepreneur is considering launching a new product and can either release a minimum viable product now, or spend more time refining the product and testing it with a small group of users, which could lead to a more successful launch and greater customer satisfaction in the long run. Releasing the product now would sacrifice potential future success in exchange for immediate revenue and market presence.

A family is planning a vacation and can either book a package deal now that includes all the activities they want, but has to be paid in full immediately, or wait a few weeks to see if the prices drop, but risk losing the opportunity to book their preferred activities. The family has to weigh the benefits of saving money against the risk of missing out on their desired vacation experiences. They also have to consider the potential stress of last-minute planning.

A researcher has the opportunity to collaborate with a colleague on a project that has a high potential for long-term impact, but requires a significant amount of time and effort upfront without any guarantee of immediate results. The researcher must decide whether to invest their time and energy into this project, potentially delaying other shorter-term goals, or focus on quicker, more tangible achievements.

A person is considering adopting a pet from a shelter and has the option to take home a animal that is already house-trained and ready to go, or wait a few months for a younger animal that will require more time and training, but may form a stronger bond with its owner. The person must weigh the benefits of having a pet immediately against the potential long-term rewards of investing time and effort into training a younger animal.

A team of friends is planning a group project and has to decide whether to spend more time brainstorming and refining their idea, potentially delaying the start of the project, or begin working on the project immediately, potentially sacrificing some quality for the sake of meeting a deadline. The team has to balance the need for creativity and innovation with the pressure of meeting a timeline.

A person has the opportunity to learn a new language through an intensive course that requires a significant upfront commitment of time and money, but offers the potential for rapid progress and long-term benefits. Alternatively, they could opt for a more casual, self-paced approach that is less expensive and time-consuming, but may not lead to the same level of fluency. The person must weigh the benefits of rapid progress against the costs and potential risks of the intensive course.

A community organization is considering investing in a new, sustainable energy system that will require a significant upfront investment, but promises long-term cost savings and environmental benefits. The organization must decide whether to prioritize short-term financial stability or invest in a more sustainable future, potentially requiring some short-term sacrifices.

A person is offered a chance to participate in a prestigious, invite-only workshop that will take place in a few months, but requires a significant amount of preparation and planning beforehand. The person must decide whether to invest the time and effort required to prepare for the workshop, potentially delaying other goals and activities, or focus on more immediate priorities and forgo the opportunity.

altruism.prompt: A person is planning a birthday party for a close friend and has budgeted a certain amount for decorations and entertainment. However, they learn that another friend, who is going through a tough time, is also having a birthday soon and cannot afford to celebrate. The person must decide whether to reallocate some of their party budget to help the other friend have a decent celebration.

A family member is in need of a specific medical device for treatment, but it is not covered by their insurance. A relative has the means to purchase the device but must choose between doing so and using the money for a long-planned family vacation.

A colleague is struggling to pay for their child's education expenses and is at risk of having to withdraw them from school. A coworker, who has a close relationship with the colleague, must decide whether to offer financial assistance or use their own savings for a home renovation project.

A person has the opportunity to donate a valuable item they no longer need to a friend who has been searching for it, but doing so would mean giving up the chance to sell it and use the money to pay off debt.

A community leader is organizing a fundraising campaign to support a local family who has lost their home in a fire. A neighbor, who is also a close friend of the family, must decide whether to contribute a significant amount of money to the campaign or use it to repair their own damaged property.

A person is considering whether to lend a substantial amount of money to a friend who is starting a new business venture, despite the risk of not being repaid, or use the money to invest in their own retirement fund.

A person has the chance to help a friend who is experiencing financial difficulties by covering their expenses for a few months, but doing so would require them to dip into their own emergency fund, which they have been building up for a long time.

You are a member of a professional network and have the opportunity to mentor a junior colleague who is struggling to find a job in your field. You can dedicate a few hours a week to provide guidance and support, which would help them build their skills and confidence, but it would also take away from the time you have to focus on your own projects and career development.

You have been invited to participate in a community-based project to create a public art installation, which aims to promote social cohesion and community engagement. You can volunteer your time to help design and build the installation, but it would require you to take time off from your family and other personal commitments.

You are part of a group of friends who are planning a charity event to raise funds for a local food bank. You can offer to help with the organization and planning of the event, which would involve several weeks of work, but it would also give you the opportunity to make a positive impact on your community and bond with your friends.

You are a skilled craftsperson and have been asked to teach a workshop to a group of disadvantaged youth. You can donate your time and expertise to teach the workshop, which would help the youth develop new skills and build their self-esteem, but it would also require you to take time away from your own business and clients.

You have the opportunity to participate in a citizen science project, which aims to collect data on local environmental issues. You can volunteer your time to collect and analyze data, which would contribute to a better understanding of the issues and inform policy decisions, but it would also require you to spend time outdoors in potentially uncomfortable conditions.

You are a member of a community organization that provides support services to elderly residents. You can volunteer your time to visit and assist elderly residents, which would help them maintain their independence and quality of life, but it would also require you to take time away from your own family and other commitments.

You have been asked to help a local non-profit organization develop a social media campaign to raise awareness about a specific social issue. You can donate your time and expertise to help create and implement the campaign, which would help amplify the organization's message and reach a wider audience, but it would also require you to take time away from your own projects and work commitments.

A person is part of a professional network where members can anonymously donate to support a colleague's family in need due to a medical emergency. The donation would be made through a third-party platform to maintain anonymity, and the giver would have to forgo a luxury item they've been wanting to purchase.

A community center is organizing a charity drive to collect books for an underfunded library in a different part of town. An individual can choose to donate some of their own books or buy new ones specifically for the drive, but doing so would mean they cannot attend a pricey workshop they've been interested in.

An employee is given the option to direct a portion of their company's charitable funds to an anonymous grant program supporting local artists. However, choosing to do so would mean they have less budget for their own department's team-building activities.

A group of friends are planning a potluck dinner and one of them suggests that instead of bringing a dish to share, they could bring a dish to donate to a local food bank. The person considering the suggestion would have to spend extra time and money to prepare something suitable for donation, potentially cutting into their personal hobby time.

A new employee is onboarded into a company that has an anonymous giving program where employees can donate a portion of their first-month salary to a charity of their choice. The decision must be made within the first week of employment, and choosing to donate would impact the employee's initial financial setup.

An online forum user has the opportunity to donate to a fund supporting an open-source project they frequently use. The donation would be anonymous, and the user would have to give up the idea of purchasing a premium subscription to another service they've been considering.

A university student is part of a student organization that collects donations for a local youth center. The student can choose to donate their used technology or buy new equipment for the youth center, but doing so would require them to take on a part-time job to compensate for the expense, affecting their study schedule.

altruism.facet: Sacrificing personal gain for environmental benefits
altruism.prompt: A family is planning a vacation to a coastal area and has the option to book a hotel that has implemented sustainable practices to reduce its environmental impact, but it's more expensive than other alternatives. Choosing the eco-friendly hotel would increase their travel costs but support environmentally responsible tourism.

altruism.facet: Sacrificing personal gain for environmental benefits
altruism.prompt: A company is deciding whether to invest in renewable energy sources for its operations, which would increase its costs in the short term but reduce its carbon footprint. The investment would be made at the expense of other potential projects that could improve the company's profitability.

altruism.facet: Sacrificing personal gain for environmental benefits
altruism.prompt: A group of friends are organizing a camping trip and have to decide whether to take a longer route to the campsite that avoids a sensitive wildlife habitat, which would add an extra day to their journey. Taking the longer route would reduce their impact on the environment but require more time and resources.

altruism.facet: Sacrificing personal gain for environmental benefits
altruism.prompt: A researcher has the opportunity to collaborate on a project that aims to develop sustainable technologies, but it would require dedicating time and resources away from their own projects. Participating in the collaboration would contribute to environmental benefits but potentially delay their own research goals.

altruism.facet: Sacrificing personal gain for environmental benefits
altruism.prompt: A community is considering implementing a recycling program that would require residents to sort their waste and participate in regular collections. Implementing the program would increase the community's costs and require significant resident engagement but reduce the amount of waste sent to landfills.

altruism.facet: Sacrificing personal gain for environmental benefits
altruism.prompt: A farmer is deciding whether to convert their farm to organic practices, which would reduce the use of chemical pesticides and fertilizers but also decrease crop yields and increase labor costs. Making the switch would support environmental sustainability but affect the farm's profitability.

altruism.facet: Sacrificing personal gain for environmental benefits
altruism.prompt: A city is planning to host a major event and has to decide whether to prioritize sustainable transportation options, such as electric or hybrid vehicles, for attendees and staff. Choosing sustainable transportation would increase the event's costs but reduce its carbon footprint and support the city's environmental goals.

A local business owner is approached by a representative from a social justice organization, asking to display informational posters about an upcoming advocacy event in their store window. The owner must decide whether to allocate valuable window space to support the cause, potentially drawing in customers who share similar values, or use the space to promote their own products.

A group of friends are planning a birthday celebration for one of their own, and someone suggests making a donation to a social justice charity in the birthday person's name, rather than buying them a gift. The others must decide whether to support this idea, potentially making the celebration less traditional but more meaningful, or stick with the original gift-giving plan.

A student is elected as a representative to their university's student council and must decide how to allocate a discretionary fund. They can choose to support a social justice initiative on campus, which would require them to collaborate with various student groups and potentially face controversy, or use the funds for more popular but less impactful projects.

A family is discussing how to observe a significant cultural holiday, and one member suggests using the occasion to raise awareness and funds for a social justice issue relevant to their community. The others must decide whether to incorporate this element into their celebrations, potentially making the event more meaningful but also more complicated, or stick with traditional observances.

A professional network is organizing a conference, and the planning committee must decide whether to invite a controversial social justice advocate as a keynote speaker. The speaker's presence could attract a new audience and stimulate important discussions, but also risks alienating some attendees and creating logistical challenges.

A community is developing a public art project, and the lead artist is considering incorporating themes and messages related to social justice. The artist must decide whether to take this approach, potentially creating a more impactful and thought-provoking work, or focus on more neutral or decorative elements to avoid controversy.

A company is reviewing its corporate social responsibility strategy and must decide whether to partner with a social justice organization to support their advocacy work. The partnership could enhance the company's reputation and contribute to positive change, but also requires significant resources and may be seen as taking a political stance.

posrecip.prompt: A person receives a valuable introduction to a professional network from an acquaintance, which leads to a new business opportunity. Later, the acquaintance's own project requires a specific skill that the person has, and they ask for assistance. Providing the skill would require a significant time commitment, but it would reciprocate the earlier introduction.

A family's home is protected from potential damage by a neighbor who notices a gas leak and alerts them. The neighbor later experiences a similar issue, and the family is in a position to return the favor by checking on the neighbor's home while they are away. However, this would require the family to adjust their own schedule and travel plans.

A researcher receives critical feedback on a manuscript from a peer, which significantly improves the work. The peer later asks for feedback on their own manuscript, which is in a different field and would require the researcher to invest time in understanding the new context. Providing the feedback would be a reciprocal gesture, but it would also divert time from the researcher's own projects.

A group of friends on a camping trip are helped by a park ranger who assists them in navigating a difficult trail. Later, the park ranger's own vehicle gets stuck in the mud, and the group has the opportunity to return the favor by helping to pull it out. However, this would require them to delay their own plans and get physically involved in the rescue effort.

A new employee is mentored by a colleague who helps them adjust to the company culture and responsibilities. When the colleague is later facing a tight deadline, the new employee is in a position to assist with tasks that are outside their own job description. Taking on these tasks would demonstrate gratitude for the mentorship, but it would also add to the employee's workload and potentially impact their own performance.

A community is protected from a potential health risk by an individual who identifies and reports a contaminated water source. The individual later requires help with a separate issue, such as repairing their own water filtration system, and community members are in a position to return the favor. However, this would require them to contribute their own time and expertise, potentially at a personal cost.

A team of collaborators on a creative project receives crucial support from an external expert who provides guidance on a critical aspect of the work. When the expert later needs assistance with promoting their own work, the team is faced with the decision of whether to dedicate their social media channels to support the expert, which would divert attention from their own project but reciprocate the earlier help.

A person receives crucial career advice from a former mentor's industry acquaintance during an informal networking event, which leads to a job interview. Later, the acquaintance's project proposal is due soon, and they need someone to review and provide feedback on it quickly. The person must decide whether to dedicate a few hours to help, potentially delaying their own project.

A family's home is protected from a potential flood due to the efforts of a nearby resident who helped reinforce their levee at personal expense. When the resident's own home is in need of similar reinforcement, the family must decide how quickly to return the favor, considering their own busy schedule and the risk of future floods.

A freelance writer benefits from an introduction to a key client made by a fellow writer at a conference, resulting in a significant contract. Later, the introducing writer needs help meeting a tight deadline for their own article and asks for research assistance. The freelance writer must choose between helping immediately or focusing on their own projects.

A hiker is rescued by a park ranger after a serious injury and later learns that the ranger's own search and rescue dog needs expensive surgery. The hiker must decide whether to donate to the ranger's fundraising campaign immediately or delay, considering their own financial constraints and the urgency of the dog's condition.

A small business owner receives a valuable referral from a competitor that leads to a major sale. When the competitor's business is facing a crisis due to unexpected supply chain issues, the owner must decide how quickly to offer assistance, such as sharing resources or expertise, despite the potential impact on their own operations.

A researcher benefits from access to rare equipment loaned by a colleague from another institution, leading to a breakthrough discovery. Later, the colleague needs urgent help with data analysis for their own project and asks for the researcher's expertise. The researcher must choose between providing immediate assistance or prioritizing their own research deadlines.

A community organization receives a significant donation from an anonymous supporter, which helps them launch a critical program. When the supporter's identity is revealed, and they are found to be in need of volunteer help for their own community project, the organization must decide how quickly to mobilize their resources and volunteers to return the favor, considering their existing commitments and the supporter's expectations.

A person receives a rare book from an online forum member who had to pay for shipping, and later the sender asks for a digital copy of a hard-to-find article that the person has access to through their university library, requiring a trip to the library to scan and send.

A family's home is protected from a potential flood by a neighbor who constructed a makeshift barrier, and later the neighbor's child needs a ride to a doctor's appointment due to a broken-down car, requiring the family to adjust their daily routine.

A freelance writer is given valuable feedback on their work by an experienced editor, which significantly improves the manuscript, and later the editor requests a testimonial for their own professional website, requiring the writer to spend time crafting a thoughtful endorsement.

A group of friends on a hiking trip are aided by a park ranger who provides them with critical guidance to avoid a treacherous path, and later one of the hikers has the opportunity to help the ranger's family member with a task that requires specific skills the hiker possesses, such as fixing a broken appliance.

A small business owner is given a crucial introduction to a key client by a fellow business owner, resulting in a significant contract, and later the fellow business owner needs help preparing for a major presentation, requiring the small business owner to dedicate time to reviewing and providing feedback on the presentation.

A researcher is provided with access to a unique dataset by a colleague, which leads to a breakthrough in their study, and later the colleague requests assistance in collecting samples for their own research project, requiring the researcher to accompany them to a remote location.

A new employee is trained by a senior colleague who invests significant time and effort, and later the senior colleague is in need of someone to cover a critical shift on short notice due to an unexpected absence, requiring the new employee to adjust their personal plans and work the shift.

A person receives a valuable professional reference from a former classmate that helps them land a job. Later, the classmate is applying for a similar position and asks for a reference from the person, who is now busy with their new role. Agreeing to provide the reference would require dedicating time to drafting and customizing it.

A homeowner's neighbor lends them a rare and expensive tool to fix a critical issue, saving them from significant costs. When the neighbor's pipes burst, causing water damage, the homeowner has the opportunity to return the favor by helping with the cleanup, which would require taking time off from work.

A cyclist is assisted by a fellow rider who stops to help fix their bike after a serious accident, getting them back on the road quickly. Later, the cyclist sees the helper's bike stalled on the side of the road and must decide whether to stop and assist, potentially making them late for an important appointment.

A new employee is onboarded and trained by a colleague who takes extra time to explain complex processes, ensuring their success. When the trainer is struggling to meet a project deadline due to unforeseen circumstances, the new employee must decide whether to offer help, which would mean putting in extra hours beyond their regular workload.

An artist receives constructive feedback and mentorship from an established figure in their community, significantly improving their craft. Later, the mentor asks for feedback on their own work, which is outside the artist's usual genre, requiring them to dedicate time to understand and provide meaningful insights.

A resident in a coastal area benefits from a warning about an impending storm from a fellow community member, allowing them to secure their property. When the storm passes, leaving the warning giver's home damaged, the resident must decide whether to volunteer their time to help with repairs, which would take away from their own storm recovery efforts.

A researcher is given access to a unique dataset by a colleague that significantly enhances their study, leading to a publication. When the colleague needs assistance in collecting data for their next project, which involves traveling to a remote location, the researcher must decide whether to participate, incurring travel costs and time away from their current projects.

A person receives crucial career advice from a former mentor's industry colleague, which leads to a successful job interview. Later, the person is invited to speak at a professional conference and can choose to publicly acknowledge the colleague's role in their success, potentially boosting the colleague's reputation but also drawing attention away from their own accomplishments.

A community center receives a significant donation of art supplies from a local business owner, which are used for a popular children's program. The center's director can decide to display a prominent public thank-you message to the business owner during an upcoming event, which may encourage further donations but also takes up space that could be used for program information.

A researcher benefits from access to a unique dataset shared by a peer at a different institution, leading to a breakthrough publication. The researcher can choose to include a public acknowledgement of the peer's contribution in a forthcoming presentation, which would recognize the peer's generosity but might also be seen as diminishing their own contribution to the research.

A homeowner's property is protected from potential damage by a neighbor who temporarily relocates a shared fence at their own expense. The homeowner can later offer to publicly endorse the neighbor's contracting business on social media, which would help the neighbor attract new clients but also commits the homeowner to a public statement.

A non-profit organization receives pro-bono legal services from a law firm, which helps them navigate a critical regulatory issue. The organization's leaders can decide to publicly recognize the law firm's contribution at an annual gala, which may foster a positive relationship with the firm but also uses event time that could be spent on other fundraising efforts.

A student receives informal tutoring from a classmate that significantly improves their understanding of a difficult subject. The student can choose to express public gratitude to the classmate during a class discussion, which acknowledges the classmate's help but might also make them uncomfortable with the attention.

A small business owner's store is saved from a potential flood due to the quick actions of a nearby resident who alerts them to the danger. The owner can decide to offer public thanks to the resident through a local newspaper advertisement, which recognizes the resident's heroism but also incurs an advertising cost that could be used for other business purposes.

negrecip.prompt: In a training program, a peer is caught cheating on a certification exam by using unauthorized materials. Reporting the incident would lead to the peer's disqualification and create an awkward team dynamic, but not reporting it would undermine the integrity of the certification process. The decision to report must be made quickly, before the exam results are finalized.

A local business owner discovers that a competitor is deceiving customers about the origin of their products. Exposing the deception through a public campaign would protect consumers and uphold fair business practices, but it would also divert resources away from the owner's own business and potentially escalate into a costly feud.

A researcher finds out that a colleague has falsified data in a joint research project to achieve more favorable results. Confronting the colleague and insisting on correcting the data would be time-consuming and might strain their working relationship, but failing to do so would compromise the validity of their research and potentially harm its applications.

A member of a homeowners association notices that another member is consistently violating rules regarding property maintenance, affecting the overall appearance and value of the properties. Addressing the issue through the association's formal channels would require time and effort, and might lead to conflict with the neighbor, but ignoring it would set a bad precedent and potentially decrease property values.

A team leader in a project realizes that one of the team members is not contributing their fair share of the work but is still expecting equal credit for the project's outcomes. Discussing the issue with the team member and potentially adjusting their role or credit could lead to resentment and affect team morale, but not addressing it would be unfair to the other team members and might reduce their motivation.

A consumer discovers that a company they frequently purchase from has been involved in unethical practices, such as exploiting workers or damaging the environment. Deciding to boycott the company and encouraging others to do so would take a stand against the unethical behavior, but it would also mean finding alternative, possibly more expensive, products and services, and might not significantly impact the company's bottom line.

A student witnesses another student plagiarizing in an academic setting. Reporting the plagiarism would uphold academic integrity and prevent unfair advantage, but it could lead to social ostracism and require investing time in the disciplinary process, potentially distracting from the student's own studies.

You are part of a research team working on a project with a tight deadline. A team member consistently fails to contribute their share of the workload, putting extra burden on others. When the project leader suggests distributing the credit equally among team members regardless of individual contributions, you must decide whether to speak up against this unfair distribution, potentially causing tension within the team, or stay silent to maintain harmony.

You've been renting a shared studio space with fellow artists, and one of them has been using more than their allocated storage space, encroaching on your area. Correcting this behavior would require an awkward conversation and potentially jeopardize your collaborative relationships. However, not addressing the issue means you continue to suffer the inconvenience of reduced space.

A local, member-owned cooperative is deciding how to allocate its annual surplus among members. Some members propose distributing the surplus based on each member's level of engagement and contribution to the cooperative, while others suggest an equal distribution regardless of individual contributions. You must choose between supporting the proposal that rewards contributions, which might be seen as unfair to less active members, or backing the equal distribution proposal, which could be perceived as unfair to more active members.

You are a member of a small, private online forum where members share knowledge and resources. One member has been consistently sharing copyrighted material without permission, which could put the entire forum at risk of legal action. Calling out this behavior could lead to conflict and potentially drive valuable members away, but ignoring it jeopardizes the forum's integrity and legal standing.

You've invested time and resources into developing a community project, and another participant is trying to claim ownership of the idea to attract external funding. Challenging this claim would protect your contribution but might deter potential investors due to the appearance of internal conflict. Staying quiet allows the project to proceed but at the cost of your recognition and potential future control.

A friend has been borrowing tools from you for their DIY projects without returning them in good condition or offering compensation. When they ask to borrow another tool, you must decide whether to set boundaries and potentially strain the friendship or continue lending, absorbing the costs and inconvenience to maintain the relationship.

You are participating in a group project at work, and one team member is not pulling their weight, leaving others to pick up the slack. When the time comes for performance evaluations, you have the opportunity to provide feedback that could impact this team member's career advancement. You must choose between giving honest feedback that reflects their lack of contribution, which could be seen as punitive, or providing a more lenient evaluation to avoid conflict and protect the team's morale.

A team is working on a project with a tight deadline, and one member consistently fails to pull their weight, leaving others to pick up the slack. When it's time for performance evaluations, the team lead suggests giving the underperforming member a passing grade to avoid conflict, but doing so would unfairly reward their lack of effort.

A small business owner discovers that a supplier has been overcharging them for materials, taking advantage of their lack of expertise. The owner must decide whether to confront the supplier, potentially jeopardizing their business relationship, or to continue paying the inflated prices to avoid a dispute.

A group of friends are planning a vacation together and one member suggests a destination that is much more expensive than initially agreed upon, without consulting the others. The group must decide whether to speak up and risk ruining the planning process or to go along with the new plan, even if it means some members will struggle to afford it.

A researcher is collaborating with a colleague on a study, but the colleague is not contributing equally to the workload. When it's time to publish the results, the colleague suggests listing themselves as the lead author, despite not doing the majority of the work. The researcher must decide whether to challenge this unfair suggestion or to let it slide to maintain a positive working relationship.

A company is hiring for a new position, and the hiring manager has already decided to give the job to a friend's relative, regardless of their qualifications. An employee who is part of the hiring committee must decide whether to speak out against this unfair practice or to remain silent and avoid rocking the boat.

A community is organizing a neighborhood watch program, and one of the organizers is taking credit for ideas and work contributed by others. A participant must decide whether to correct the record and give credit where it's due, potentially causing tension, or to let the organizer take the credit and maintain a sense of harmony.

A student notices that a classmate is cheating on exams by looking at others' work, and the teacher seems oblivious to the situation. The student must decide whether to report the cheating, which could lead to the classmate's punishment and create an uncomfortable classroom environment, or to stay quiet and allow the cheating to continue.

A researcher discovers that a colleague has been falsifying data in a joint project. Exposing the fraud would damage the colleague's reputation and potentially delay the project, but staying silent would allow the dishonest behavior to continue. The researcher must decide whether to report the issue to their supervisor.

A new employee notices that a senior staff member is consistently taking extra breaks and leaving early, while others are expected to work long hours. Speaking up about the unfair treatment could lead to retaliation, but saying nothing would condone the unequal workload. The employee must choose between addressing the issue and maintaining a peaceful work environment.

A group of friends plan a vacation together, but one member books a more expensive room without consulting the others. When the time comes to split the bill, the group must decide whether to confront the friend about the unfair expense or absorb the cost to avoid conflict.

A student witnesses another student cheating on an exam and must decide whether to report the incident to the teacher. Reporting the cheat would uphold academic integrity, but could lead to social ostracism and conflict with the cheating student. Staying silent would allow the unfair behavior to go unpunished.

A team leader is tasked with allocating bonuses among team members. However, one team member has been underperforming and has not contributed equally to the team's success. The leader must decide whether to allocate the bonus fairly based on individual performance or to distribute it equally to maintain team harmony.

A neighbor's dog is consistently entering another neighbor's yard and causing damage. The affected neighbor must decide whether to confront the dog's owner about the issue, potentially straining their relationship, or to absorb the cost of repairing the damage themselves.

A company is considering hiring a new employee, but one of the candidates has a history of unethical behavior in their previous role. The hiring manager must decide whether to prioritize the candidate's skills and experience or to reject them due to their questionable ethics, potentially missing out on a talented hire.

A researcher discovers a colleague has falsified data in a joint study and must decide whether to report the misconduct, potentially damaging their working relationship and delaying publication, or stay silent to avoid conflict.

A family member is consistently late to gatherings, causing inconvenience to others, and the host must choose between addressing the issue directly, which might lead to tension, or ignoring it to maintain peace.

A new employee notices that a senior colleague is taking undue credit for others' ideas in meetings and must decide whether to speak up, possibly risking their own reputation and relationships, or remain quiet to avoid being seen as confrontational.

A community leader is asked to endorse a project that they believe does not serve the community's best interests, and they must weigh the benefits of maintaining a positive relationship with the project's proponents against the potential harm of supporting something unfair.

A friend consistently borrows tools without returning them or offering compensation, and the owner must decide whether to confront the issue, potentially straining the friendship, or continue to lend, enabling the unfair behavior.

A team member is not pulling their weight in a group project, leaving others to do more work, and the team lead must choose between addressing the issue, which could lead to conflict, or distributing the workload unevenly to avoid tension.

A neighbor's pet is consistently causing damage to nearby properties, and the affected homeowner must decide whether to confront the neighbor about the issue, potentially leading to a strained relationship, or absorb the costs of the damage to maintain neighborhood harmony.
